From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Super-featherweight](/w/index.php?title=Super-featherweight&redirect=no "Super-featherweight"))

Weight division in professional boxing: 57–59 kg

**Super featherweight**, also known as **junior lightweight**, is a [weight division](/wiki/Boxing_weight_classes "Boxing weight classes") in [professional boxing](/wiki/Professional_boxing "Professional boxing"), contested between 126 pounds (57 kg) and 130 pounds (59 kg).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Super_featherweight&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The super-featherweight division appeared in two distinct historical periods, from 1921 to 1934 and 1949 to the present. This weight class was established by the New York [Walker Law](/wiki/Walker_Law "Walker Law") in 1920, known as the junior-lightweight division. In 1921, [Johnny Dundee](/wiki/Johnny_Dundee "Johnny Dundee") would defeat [George Chaney](/wiki/George_Chaney "George Chaney") by a fifth-round DQ to become the division's first universally recognized world champion, as acknowledged by the [New York State Athletic Commission](/wiki/New_York_State_Athletic_Commission "New York State Athletic Commission") (NYSAC).[[1]](#cite_note-1) In 1924, [Steve Sullivan](/wiki/Steve_Sullivan_(boxer) "Steve Sullivan (boxer)") would then defeat the reigning Dundee by a 10-round UD to win *[The Ring](/wiki/The_Ring_(magazine) "The Ring (magazine)")* magazine's inaugural title in the division.[[2]](#cite_note-boxrec-ring-2) In 1927, the [National Boxing Association](/wiki/National_Boxing_Association "National Boxing Association") (NBA) (the direct predecessor to the [World Boxing Association](/wiki/World_Boxing_Association "World Boxing Association") (WBA)), awarded their inaugural title to reigning world champion [Tod Morgan](/wiki/Tod_Morgan "Tod Morgan").[[3]](#cite_note-WBA-3)

The NYSAC abolished the division in 1930 due to suspicions of fight-fixing after [Benny Bass](/wiki/Benny_Bass "Benny Bass") won the title from [Tod Morgan](/wiki/Tod_Morgan "Tod Morgan") the previous year, a fight in which Morgan dominated the first round before suffering a sudden second-round stoppage, all after bookmakers had placed unusually high odds on him to win just before the fight began.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) The NBA and *The Ring* both upheld Bass' win, and the division continued to be recognized at least outside of New York. However, during the reign of [Kid Chocolate](/wiki/Kid_Chocolate "Kid Chocolate"), *The Ring* discontinued its junior-lightweight ratings in 1931, with the NBA initially following suit in 1932.[[2]](#cite_note-boxrec-ring-2)[[3]](#cite_note-WBA-3) Kid Chocolate would be briefly reinstated as champion by the NBA in 1933, which continued to recognize the division until [Frankie Klick](/wiki/Frankie_Klick "Frankie Klick") vacated the title in 1934.[[3]](#cite_note-WBA-3) After this, the division was largely abandoned until 1949, when [Sandy Saddler](/wiki/Sandy_Saddler "Sandy Saddler") won the vacant NBA junior-lightweight title after defeating Orlando Zulueta by a 10-round SD, continuing to hold on to the title until 1957 to stay at featherweight.[[3]](#cite_note-WBA-3)

In 1962, reigning WBA[[a]](#cite_note-6) champion [Flash Elorde](/wiki/Flash_Elorde "Flash Elorde") was named by *The Ring* magazine as junior-lightweight champion of the world, 31 years after they had initially withdrawn recognition of the division.[[2]](#cite_note-boxrec-ring-2) He would then go on to win the inaugural [World Boxing Council](/wiki/World_Boxing_Council "World Boxing Council") (WBC) super-featherweight title in 1963 by defeating Johnny Bizarro by a 15-round UD.[[6]](#cite_note-7) The inaugural champion for the [International Boxing Federation](/wiki/International_Boxing_Federation "International Boxing Federation") (IBF) was [Hwan-Kil Yuh](/wiki/Hwan-Kil_Yuh "Hwan-Kil Yuh") in 1984, who won the title by defeating Rod Sequenan via a 15-round SD.[[7]](#cite_note-8) The first [World Boxing Organization](/wiki/World_Boxing_Organization "World Boxing Organization") champion was [John John Molina](/wiki/John_John_Molina "John John Molina") in 1989, defeating [Juan Laporte](/wiki/Juan_Laporte "Juan Laporte") by a 12-round UD to win the title.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

Some other notable fighters to hold championship titles at this weight include [Brian Mitchell](/wiki/Brian_Mitchell_(boxer) "Brian Mitchell (boxer)"), [Arturo Gatti](/wiki/Arturo_Gatti "Arturo Gatti"), [Vasiliy Lomachenko](/wiki/Vasiliy_Lomachenko "Vasiliy Lomachenko"), [Alexis Argüello](/wiki/Alexis_Arg%C3%BCello "Alexis Argüello"), [Azumah Nelson](/wiki/Azumah_Nelson "Azumah Nelson"), [Julio César Chávez](/wiki/Julio_C%C3%A9sar_Ch%C3%A1vez "Julio César Chávez"), [Diego Corrales](/wiki/Diego_Corrales "Diego Corrales"), [Floyd Mayweather Jr.](/wiki/Floyd_Mayweather_Jr. "Floyd Mayweather Jr."), [Érik Morales](/wiki/%C3%89rik_Morales "Érik Morales"), [Marco Antonio Barrera](/wiki/Marco_Antonio_Barrera "Marco Antonio Barrera"), [Acelino Freitas](/wiki/Acelino_Freitas "Acelino Freitas"), [Juan Manuel Márquez](/wiki/Juan_Manuel_M%C3%A1rquez "Juan Manuel Márquez"), [Oscar De La Hoya](/wiki/Oscar_De_La_Hoya "Oscar De La Hoya"), [Rocky Lockridge](/wiki/Rocky_Lockridge "Rocky Lockridge"), and [Manny Pacquiao](/wiki/Manny_Pacquiao "Manny Pacquiao").
